Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 159 It had no value on its own, but it was almost always decisive: the one card that could turn around the whole game, and alter existing values, either by a nullifying or multiplying operation. In a technical sense the fool has no trick-making power: in most games (including tarot) it may be played at any time, irrespective of any obligation to follow suit or to play a trump. There can be little doubt that it was very much via card playing that the trickster figure diffused into popular culture in Europe. Strategically speaking, playing cards was of course very much about ‘tricking’ (and outright cheating – something almost impossible in chess and difficult with dice). Third, the practice of card playing introduced a completely new ‘social setting’ in a very concrete sense: four persons sitting around a square table. All early card games were played by four persons. European decks have four ‘types’ or ‘suits’, and the number of cards is always divisible by four (not three or five). This is a structural property of practically all the first card decks produced in Europe. The last thing to take notice of in this context is that very often two of the players were women. Judging from the available paintings, images and written records, card playing was particularly attractive for young women – and the playing of cards became connected to public flirting and sexual seduction. It was very much via card playing that women were drawn or ‘seduced’ into the public sphere. Fourth, card playing opened up whole new possibilities for gambling practices, ways of calculating and counting up wins and losses; and most of today’s gambling practices derive directly from cards. Venice and Institutionalized Gambling: The World’s First Casino It is therefore not surprising that in 1638 the first official, permanent casino was inaugurated in Venice, in the middle of the Thirty Years’ War. It went under the name, Il Ridotto. The Ridotto was important for two reasons: it was the world’s first government-sanctioned gambling house, and the first that was open to the general public. In short, the Ridotto makes Venice the birthplace of the casino. Il Ridotto is a wing of Venice’s San Moisè Palace, a nobleman’s villa and palace at the Campo San Moisè. 8 This was the first legal, public mercantile Casino ever. It was here that Casanova would often seduce women of the Venetian aristocracy. Placed in a palace named after Moses, 9 on a road close to the Canal Grande – in fact, just at the entrance of the Canal Grande – gambling became institutionalized. Casinos, 8 The building has now turned into a 3 star hotel. Visitors who stay there get free tickets to the casino. 9 Not a few ironies are at play here; the casino was also close to the Church of San Moisè (on Piazza San Marco), and next door to the Theatre of San Moisè. Moses is the figure of origin of Judaic and Christian morality and ethics, as we trace the Ten Commandments back to him. If we consider some of the commandments (thou shalt not steal; thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s house; thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that is thy neighbour’s) casino Liminality and the Modern 160 of course, must in and by themselves be seen as quintessentially liminal spaces, set apart from ordinary rules and norms for behaviour: an out-of-the-ordinary space where excitement thrives. The term ‘ridotto’ comes from the Italian word ‘riddure’, meaning to ‘close off’ or ‘make private’. It originally referred to several illegal, privately owned gambling clubs that offered games of chance to members of Venice’s nobility in the city’s Rialto district. The reference to the ‘private room’ is doubly fitting, as it also indicates the fact that prostitution flourished alongside the gambling activities (a persistent combination ever since). These clubs came into being after the Venetian authorities attempted to ban games of chance that had spontaneously sprung up in the city’s streets. Realizing that it could not effectively prevent citizens from wagering on dice and card games, the Great Council of Venice opened its Ridotto in 1638. Not surprisingly, they did so in a liminal moment: on the occasion of the city’s annual spring carnival. The world’s first casino was thus established to provide controlled gambling during the carnival season, but soon started to run for many months of the year – and this of course happened as the carnival developed into something like an almost permanent institution in Venice. Due to the high stakes and formal dress code, only nobles could afford to play at the casino’s tables; one such discriminating injunction, for instance, was that players had to wear three-cornered hats and masks in order to participate in Il Ridotto’s games. The casino was closed in the 1770s as the city government perceived it to impoverish the local gentry, that was gambling away its shrinking fortunes. In 1774 Venetian reformer Giorgio Pisani proposed to close the Ridotto ‘to preserve the piety, sound discipline and moderate behaviour’ of the city’s inhabitants. Pisani’s motion passed by an overwhelming majority and the casino closed its doors the same year. By then, however, the gambling genie had long since left the bottle; and the carnival had become a near-permanent state. This was not just a Venetian development: from the eighteenth century, professional gambling houses and lotteries started to spread across Europe. In a general vein, the carnivalesque, with its hybridization, mixing of codes, grotesque bodies and transgressions, remained a fascinating spectacle for eighteenth century writers.
